In business deals, or any deal for that matter, while a certain key individual may occupy the fabled role of decision-maker, please keep in mind they are making choices on behalf of an entire organization. They have a team, and their team is where the deal killers lurk. The deal killers are the ones affected by or critical to implementation. In order to secure and guarantee implementation, you've got to discover how to reach through your immediate counterpart and affect these key players. They are vital, because implementation is their responsibility, and implementation is where agreements are made or broken and profit is lost or gained. Even if you can't physically reach these individuals, you can still influence them. This isn't as difficult as you might expect, because interestingly enough, the vast majority of the time, they simply want to feel like they've been taken into account during the process. Their biggest motivation for breaking your deal is most often because they feel they weren't included. Not that they actually object to the deal itself, but that they weren't taken into account and therefore feel disrespected. Even nature tells us being autonomous is a top priority for people. If they don't think they were included in a decision, they may torpedo it just to maintain autonomy. Using calibrated how and what questions like, how does this affect the rest of the team? Or what do your colleagues see as their main challenges in this area? You will begin to trigger conversations behind the table on the other side, which will address the hidden deal-breaking inclinations. It's important to ask these questions more than once over the course of the negotiation. It's not that you're questioning the answers you're getting. This repetition will significantly increase the likelihood your counterpart will turn around and double-check with his or her colleagues and boom! The process of more effective implementation will have started. You will have single-handedly united the other side to come together on a decision. Deal breakers have the power to block or slow down agreements they don't like and help support ones that they do. At the Black Swan Group, we've discovered that fully 50% of the signed deals that one of the major telecommunication carriers makes are never consummated. 50% of the signed deals are killed internally. If that's the tip of the iceberg, then when it comes down to it, deal killers have as much of an impact as deal-makers. Even if you're talking directly to the CEO, you can't disregard these people. Even if you keep the needs and wants of all the players involved in mind, you're still going to come across some personalities who will make it difficult to close a deal. Unfortunately, these aggressive jerks and serial fabricators come with the territory of not just negotiating, but life itself. One thing you may find helpful to keep in mind is these actions by these fabricators are largely defense mechanisms, which means they don't know any better way to defend themselves. Let's talk first about the serial fabricators, or liars. One of the phenomenal things about focusing on implementation is it kills two birds with one stone. The first bird, those who haven't thought through implementation because they just don't think ahead. And the second bird is the serial fabricator. So whichever character you're dealing with, they won't be able to answer how and what led implementation focus questions because they haven't thought implementation through. Whether they're short-sighted or they never intended to implement in the first place, you will be met with silence. Ask, how are we going to get this done? And how is that going to be implemented? And what will happen next? Talk this all the way through to the end. Liars will be able to make up some nonsense on the spur of the moment to get them through your first implementation question. But that will wear them out so much they'll go dead silent on the second or the third. Liars are usually good at deception in one good instance at a time. Very rarely can they exhibit that type of deception consecutively in the same conversation over the same issue. Either way, you're servicing a huge disconnect without actually embarrassing anyone. The toxic residue of embarrassment is so lasting and negative that in the long run, you will come to wish it wasn't there. And you'll be living with serial fabricators. Now let's address aggressive jerks. We'll get to more techniques in the next chapter, but for now, let's stick with our good friend Hao. The way this works is like the Chinese martial art of Tai Chi. You use your opponent's aggressiveness against him or her and turn their antagonism into your way of defeating them. A great example of this would be, and by the way, here's where you can name the aggressive behavior. How are we going to solve our problems if you keep making threats? Make sure you use the late night FM DJ voice and the inflection at the end is critical, just like I did. It has to be upward and inquiring. You're going to be sorely tempted to make it downward and commanding. Please don't. If you do, they will react as if you're being condescending and the wrestling match will continue. Now pause. As necessary, repeat the question with the prescribed tonality and the effect of pause. Like it says on the back of the shampoo bottle, wash, rinse, repeat. You'll impact the person a little more each time until you convert them out of the bad behavior. They'll feel like it was their choice to stop the behavior. Therefore their adaptation will be more lasting than if you force them into it. Be prepared to reapply this tonic whenever the symptoms appear. The last thing that you want to do is go toe to toe with the aggressive type and struggle for control that only leads to a wrestling match where you're trying to force each other to submit. Instead, dissipate their threats with carefully chosen what and how questions and you'll give your counterpart the illusion of control and get them to solve the problems for you. Lastly, this charms everyone because we all love to tell people how to do things. The rest of the people you deal with will be charmed by these questions and they will feel listened to. People love that and you'll love the results.
